topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion. A profile picture I hadn't seen in fifteen years, a smirk that used to make my stomach clench with dread. It was Mark. My Mark. The kid who used to dismantle my confidence with cruel words and petty sabotage in the schoolyard. A slow, hot wave of anger washed over me, tightening my chest until it was hard to draw a full breath. I stared at the request, my jaw clenching so hard I thought my teeth might crack. What in the world was he trying to pull? I didn't even bother to open the message thread. I just deleted the request, a sharp, decisive movement, and shoved my phone into my pocket, the sudden tension in my shoulders making me want to physically lash out.

[story 2]

I nearly dropped my coffee mug when the alert chimed. It was a connection request, and the name staring back at me was a ghost from my past—Mark. My entire body seemed to seize up. A low, vibrating hum started deep in my core, a pressure building behind my eyes. I felt a sudden, intense need to slam my laptop shut, to throw the device across the room. *Why now?* The thought echoed in my head, sharp and corrosive. I took a long, ragged breath, trying to steady the tremor in my hands. I didn't want to engage. I wanted him to disappear. I navigated to the settings, my fingers moving with jerky, uncontrolled speed, and blocked the connection instantly, the silence of the room suddenly deafening after the internal storm.

[story 3]

When I saw the notification, a cold, hard knot formed in my gut. It wasn't just surprise; it was a deep, simmering resentment that instantly flooded my system. I felt a flush creep up my neck, hot and undeniable. Mark. The name alone was enough to make my skin crawl. I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles turned white, the pressure building until my vision seemed to narrow. I reread the profile—the same arrogant expression I remembered so vividly. I leaned back in my chair, letting out a slow, deliberate exhale that sounded more like a hiss. I didn't need to reply. I just needed to ensure that whatever this attempt was, it would meet a wall of absolute, unyielding silence.
